  "opinion": "I think the best way to handle fake content online is simply to ignore it most of the time, like a game where you don't play with people who lie. For instance, checking news sources carefully before believing anything is one way — but even then, the sheer amount can feel overwhelming given low conscientiousness scores mean sticking to routines isn’t easy for me.\n\nI also try to interact less online because my moderate extraversion level doesn't need constant stimulation from social media; real life hobbies like photography or birdwatching give me more authentic connections anyway. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think the best way to handle fake content online is simply to ignore it most of the time, like a game where you don't play with people who lie. It's exhausting to always fact-check everything someone posts, especially when my anxiety makes me worry about missing something important or being tricked by misinformation that could affect my mental health and daily life in Bari.

Since I'm just a full-time student with limited time on social media — probably spending it during weekends or evenings after class watching some classic music while trying to relax — fake stuff can really add stress. Sometimes people share inflammatory news hoping for engagement, but that kind of content doesn’t help me at all; my anxiety would make me want to hide from it rather than deal with it directly.

So I focus on curating my feed myself: following accounts that stick to facts and real personal stories instead of drama or rumors. For instance, checking news sources carefully before believing anything is one way — but even then, the sheer amount can feel overwhelming given low conscientiousness scores mean sticking to routines isn’t easy for me.

I also try to interact less online because my moderate extraversion level doesn't need constant stimulation from social media; real life hobbies like photography or birdwatching give me more authentic connections anyway. And honestly, when I see something suspicious? Just a shrug emoji and moving on feels best.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I decided ignoring fake content is key because my high neuroticism makes it mentally draining to constantly analyze everything for lies; plus being anxious means false information can unsettle me more than helpful truth. Low openness doesn't help with adapting new tools, but I stick to what’s familiar — like carefully vetting news sources rather than creating complex algorithms.

My low conscientiousness prevents deep dives into systematic ways of handling fake content over time; and moderate agreeableness means sometimes not calling out misinformation directly because that could stir conflict unnecessarily. Instead, focusing on personal curating fits my lifestyle better.
response_parsed.tone: Casual yet thoughtful with a hint of detachment due to high neuroticism
response_parsed.safety_notes: None needed as this doesn't involve sensitive topics or harmful content references.
